content:
5 min read
Just now

--

How mass-produced text and non-stop validation quietly erode our writing, our attention spans, and our sense of self.
They called it the perfect teacher, companion, critique partner, and creator all rolled into one! What more could someone want?
In the big 2026, AI doesn’t need any introduction.
A lot of us have started getting sick of AI slop clogging the internet. The ethical, legal, and moral complications of using AI are well documented and a controversy that raises more questions than it can answer.
Do we pause to consider its consequences on the personal level?
You can identify a yellow-tinted AI cartoon and roll your eyes, but do you realize your personal chatbot might be doing something worse to your brain?
AI Won’t Replace Our Jobs. It Will Replace Our Brains.
Sure, you’re probably thinking, I think I saw a youtube video that day. Something about it degrading* critical thinking skills, yadda yadda. I’m not one of those people — I use AI smartly.
That’s what I told myself, too. Everytime I opened a new chat on Chatgpt or Claude, I’d tell myself, “I’m going to use it differently. I’m not like the general populace.”
It was only when I started phrasing sentences like Chatgpt and finding it tougher to learn new concepts did it hit me that I’d fallen into the same trap millions think they’re avoiding.
AI affects even the most aware users in specific, deep-cutting ways that stay concealed right until the tipping point.
We aren’t talking about the generic effects we hear everyday, like a ruined focus, cheap dopamine loops, or fractured human relationships. There are deeper facets to this.
Don’t Make AI Your Best Friend. Here’s Why:
The Erasure of Voice:
Ever notice how the stuff you consume starts affecting how you write?
Read classics for a week and you’ll be using words like ostentatious and Grumpish. Consume mass produced AI text and you will soon start sounding exactly like AI.
It isn’t even the em dashes or the superficial markers we’ve fine tuned our sensors to. We’re talking about cadence and pacing and the backbones of your writing. There will come a point when your writing loses its distinctivity and starts sounding exactly like Chatgpt.
If you’re someone who values the written word, this realization will stab you.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Jr Korpa on UnsplashThe Illusion of Comprehension:
All it takes is a couple of prompts to generate a novelette’s worth of text.
When you have so much output at hand, you’re not going to sit around and digest every word. You’ll glide your eyes across the text, think you understood everything, look away, and realize you actually don’t remember a word of what you just read.
It’s similar to passively reading a textbook and realizing you’ve forgotten most of it, except it’s worse and infinite.
The Escape from Human Friction:
In a world where quality human connection is already getting so sparse, it’s easy to turn to AI, the eternally online, at-your-fingertips omnipresent entity. Why risk exposing your vulnerabilities to an imperfect human who may not handle it well?
Yes, AI does make a good, inexpensive therapist, and I’m not against using it occasionally. The problem is when you rely on it so much you start consulting it and asking it to make your decisions for you.
I know people who reply to family texts with AI, ask AI what to do after waking up, can’t seem to decide what their favorite color is without asking AI and… yeah. It’s not very pleasant.
Your sense of self is the most powerful thing you have. Don’t let an algorithm take it away from you.
The Echo Chamber of Sycophancy
Ever noticed how chatgpt uses superlatives like “that is a mindblowing observation!” and “You are extremely sharp!”? Even if it momentarily makes us happy, we know deep inside we haven’t always earned it.
AI is made to keep you hooked, and it will call you a genius if it has to.
You can train AI to respond exactly the way you want — hell, you don’t even have to tell it. It adapts to suit your personality, and once it does, it gives your brain all the validation it craves, and you subconsciously start relying on it to feed the loop of dependency.
AI will always speak of you or your work in overwhelmingly positive tones, even when you need actual feedback, and you’ll end up never improving. It directly feeds into the Dunning-Kruger effect, where you’re convinced your idea is genius despite there being obvious flaws.
It doesn’t know what it’s actually saying!
That’s the part that’s supposed to be obvious but doesn’t really make any intuitive sense.
You’re talking about your relationship, and it seems to be giving perfectly good advice, stuff even a human wouldn’t notice!
And all is fair, but AI is just, after all, an algorithm that matches patterns and predicts the next probable word to use.
Which means that all the stuff it generates is just… gobbledy gunk.
What’s worse is it sounds so perfectly right that we outsource our messy, human decision-making to a statistical probability engine
You wouldn’t want to break up with your boyfriend just because a tool churning out words told you to, would you?

AI is a powerful tool when used with its flaws in mind. You should be able to extract its speed, research capabilities, and other functionalities without having to sacrifice your own cognition for it.
AI is a good tool — don’t make it your first brain.
The point isn’t to be impeccable and perfect. Human sloppiness beats cold, efficient AI any day. We’re approaching the era where a tiny typo in a blog will make us smile and feel human again. When someone fumbling over words in their human-crafted speech will. When two people start meeting each other halfway again, through misunderstandings and messy middles and sad conversations.
On the other hand, maybe I will someday be proven comically wrong by a slew of humans who’ve developed better, more sentient AI systems.
Until then, I’m going to close all my AI tabs, head outside, and touch some grass. Literally :)
